name: assume
description: If you assume a proposition, you can deduce it ‚ÄĒ because you just said it's true.

name: ex-falso
description: From a contradiction ($\bot$), you can derive any formula $p$. The principle of explosion ‚ÄĒ *ex falso quodlibet*.

name: fix
description: Introduce a fresh term variable into the context ‚ÄĒ like assume, but for terms.

name: subst
description: Uniformly replace atoms with formulas in a proven judgement. The proof structure is independent of the particular symbols ‚ÄĒ a structural rule.
example:
1. ‚äĘ P ‚Üí P
2. (subst 1 (P (and A B)))  ‚Üí  ‚äĘ (A ‚ąß B) ‚Üí (A ‚ąß B)

name: ¬¨-elim
description: From $\lnot p$ derive $p \to \bot$ ‚ÄĒ negation unwraps into an implication to falsity.

name: ¬¨-intro
description: If assuming $p$ leads to both $q$ and $\lnot q$ (a contradiction), then $\lnot p$ must hold. *Reductio ad absurdum* ‚ÄĒ reduce to the absurd.

name: ¬¨¬¨-elim
description: From $\lnot \lnot p$ derive $p$. Eliminate a double negation ‚ÄĒ classical logic's escape hatch.

name: ‚Üí-elim
description: If $p$ implies $q$, and you have $p$, then you have $q$. The classic syllogism ‚ÄĒ *modus ponens*.
example:
1. (assume P)  ‚Üí  {P} ‚äĘ P
2. P ‚Üí Q provided as premise
3. (->-elim 2 1) ‚Üí Q

name: ‚Üí-intro
description: If from assuming $p$ you can reach $q$, then $p$ implies $q$ ‚ÄĒ and you can safely drop $p$ from the context.

name: ‚ąÄ-elim
description: From $\forall x.\; \varphi$, derive $\varphi$ with any term $t$ substituted for $x$. What holds for everything holds for any particular thing.

name: ‚ąÄ-intro
description: If you prove $\varphi$ for an arbitrary $x$ not appearing elsewhere, the proof holds for all $x$. Universal generalisation.
example:
1. (fix x)  ‚Üí  {x} ‚äĘ x
2. (assume (App P x))  ‚Üí  {x, P(x)} ‚äĘ P(x)
3. (->-intro (App P x) 2)  ‚Üí  {x} ‚äĘ P(x) ‚Üí P(x)
4. (forall-intro x 3)  ‚Üí  ‚äĘ ‚ąÄx. P(x) ‚Üí P(x)

name: ‚ąÉ-elim
description: From an existential claim, open a witness $y$, prove the conclusion $\psi$ under the assumption $\varphi[y/x]$, and discharge ‚ÄĒ provided $y$ doesn't escape into $\Delta$ or $\psi$.

name: ‚ąÉ-intro
description: If you have a concrete example $\varphi$ involving term $t$, generalise it to $\exists x.\; \varphi$ by replacing $t$ with a fresh variable $x$. From the particular to the existential.

name: ‚ąß-elim-l
description: If you know $p \land q$, then you know $p$ ‚ÄĒ the left half of the conjunction.

name: ‚ąß-elim-r
description: If you know $p \land q$, then you know $q$ ‚ÄĒ the right half of the conjunction.

name: ‚ąß-intro
description: If you've proved $p$ and you've proved $q$, then you've proved $p \land q$ ‚ÄĒ they're both true together.

name: ‚ą®-elim
description: If $p \lor q$ is true, and both $p$ and $q$ independently lead to $r$, then $r$ is true regardless. Reason by cases ‚ÄĒ exhaust the possibilities.
rule: \frac{\Gamma \vdash p \lor q \quad \Delta_1, p \vdash r \quad \Delta_2, q \vdash r}{\Gamma \cup \Delta_1 \cup \Delta_2 \vdash r}

name: ‚ą®-intro-l
description: If you've proved $p$, you've proved $p \lor q$ ‚ÄĒ because the left side is already true. The $q$ can be anything.

name: ‚ą®-intro-r
description: If you've proved $q$, you've proved $p \lor q$ ‚ÄĒ because the right side is already true. The $p$ can be anything.
